paper_title: StressDream: Steering Video World Models for Robust Policy Evaluation and Improvement

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Video world models (WMs) have shown promise for policy evaluation and improvement by imagining realistic future observations conditioned on ego-robot actions. While WMs can model distributions over futures, policy evaluation and improvement typically rely on nominal imaginations, which can miss high-impact outcomes of robot actions unless prohibitively many samples are drawn. To enable robust policy evaluation and improvement over WM imaginations, we propose StressDream, which steers imaginations toward high-impact yet plausible outcomes specified at inference time by optimizing the initial noise of diffusion-based WMs. However, optimizing high-dimensional noise is challenging: the optimization must reason about nuanced, scene-dependent target events in generated videos while avoiding out-of-distribution (OOD) noise that yields implausible imaginations. We address this with two complementary objectives: a semantic objective with a Vision-Language Model that provides informative gradients by reasoning about the generated video, and a plausibility objective that prevents the optimized noise from drifting OOD. With state-of-the-art video world models for autonomous driving and robotic manipulation, we show that StressDream effectively steers imaginations toward high-impact yet plausible outcomes specified by text at inference time, such as task failures, enabling robust policy evaluation and improvement by identifying actions whose plausible futures include undesirable outcomes. Video results are available at https://junwon.me/StressDream/.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Video world models (WMs), or learned simulators of physical environments, have attracted significant interest in robotics, promising policy evaluation and improvement with substantially less costly real-world interaction [1, 2, 3]. At their core, video WMs are generative models, such as diffusion and flow matching [4, 5, 6], learning distributions over future observations conditioned on ego robot actions, and thereby capturing the inherent uncertainty of physical interactions or behaviors of other agents [7, 8, 9, 10] (top, Fig. 1). For example, in robotic manipulation, dropping an open bag from high above the table may or may not cause its contents to spill, leading to either task success or failure; in autonomous driving, the same ego-vehicle decision can elicit qualitatively different outcomes depending on the responses of surrounding agents, such as braking or maintaining speed. Despite learning rich distributions over future outcomes, policy evaluation and improvement with WMs typically rely on nominal imaginations [8, 11, 12, 13], which can under-explore the diverse outcomes they are trained to model. Naively sampling imaginations from WMs can easily miss critical outcomes without drawing prohibitively many samples, making it ill-suited to the role of WMs: exploring plausible, high-impact outcomes of robot actions for policy evaluation and improvement [14, 15, 16, 17], where plausibility refers to outcomes supported by the learned WM distribution. For example, when manipulator drops an open bag from high above the table, the WMs distribution may include outcomes both with and without spilling; in contrast, when the bag is placed close to the table, spilling may be unlikely or absent from the outcome distribution (bottom, Fig. 1). The ability to imagine plausible, high-impact outcomes can therefore allow the latter action to be preferred during policy evaluation, while the former is downweighted or suppressed during policy improvement. This motivates the central question of our work: How can we efficiently steer video world models at inference time to imagine plausible, high-impact outcomes for policy evaluation and improvement? Our key idea is to optimize the initial noise of diffusion-based WM, steering generation toward high-impact events specified at inference time (e.g., spills or collisions), within the distribution of plausible outcomes. The challenge, however, is to guide this optimization in extremely highdimensional noise spaces (e.g., 1M dims [11]): the optimization must reason about nuanced, scenedependent target events from generated videos, while naive optimization can easily push the initial noise into out-of-distribution (OOD) regions [18, 19], producing implausible videos. To address this, we propose STRESSDREAM, which combines semantic objective, using Vision-Language Model (VLM) that reasons about what happens in the generated video to guide generation toward target events, with plausibility objective that keeps the optimized noise within the in-distribution region. Together, STRESSDREAM provides dense gradient signals for searching the high-dimensional noise space, while preserving plausibility of generations, enabling video WMs to explore plausible, highimpact outcome distributions for robust policy evaluation and improvement. Statement of Contributions. We propose STRESSDREAM, novel inference-time steering method for diffusion-based video world models. Our algorithm optimizes the high-dimensional Gaussian initial noise to steer generations toward high-impact outcomes while preserving plausibility. Through experiments, we show: (i) in controlled settings with access to the true system dynamics, STRESSDREAM steers WM imaginations toward target task-failure events only when such outcomes are plausible; (ii) on state-of-the-art video world models for autonomous driving [11] and robotic manipulation [12], STRESSDREAM enables robust policy evaluation by detecting high-impact outcomes, such as task failures, with substantially higher recall (54% 94%); and (iii) this robust evaluation improves policy by promoting robust actions that avoid potential failures, increasing the success rate of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy [20] (39% 71%).

sections.title: 2 Background: Action-conditioned Video World Models
sections.content: Video World Models. An action-conditioned video world model fθ, parameterized by θ, models distributions of future observations := ot:t+H , where denotes the time and is the prediction horizon [12, 15, 16, 21, 22, 23]. Each observation = [I 1, , n, q] consists of camera images and proprioceptive state q, such as the robot joint configuration. The prediction is conditioned on an observation history ohist := oth:t and future ego-action sequence := at:t+H : fθ( ohist, a), where := ot:t+H , ohist := oth:t, := at:t+H . Thus, all stochasticity in generation is governed by the initial noise, implying that selecting the initial noise controls which video is generated from the WMs.

sections.title: 3 Setup: Policy Evaluation and Improvement with Video World Models
sections.content: Policy Evaluation and Improvement with Video World Models. Given an action-conditioned video world model fθ, we aim to evaluate candidate action sequences by imagining their future outcomes, which can then be used for policy evaluation [15, 16, 21] and improvement [12, 13, 17]. The predicted futures are evaluated by test-time criterion function Ctest(o) R, which measures whether plausible outcomes with high impact on policy evaluation and improvement occur in the generated videos. These evaluations can be used to improve policies by selecting actions that avoid such outcomes [26, 27], or by training policy π(a ohist) using world-model imaginations [12, 13]. However, when multiple futures are possible under the same action sequence, robust policy [28, 29] should account for the distribution of outcomes that are plausible under the ego robot action: Inner opt.: high-dim. (e.g., 1M) Gaussian noise generating high-impact, yet plausible futures = argmin aA max ϵRD Ctest(cid:0)fθ(ϵ, ohist, a)(cid:1) (4) Outer opt.: action robust to the distribution over plausible futures where the inner optimization chooses Gaussian noise to steer the WM generation toward worstcase plausible future that maximizes the criterion, while the outer optimization selects robust action sequence that keeps the criterion low across plausible futures, even under worst-case outcomes. Various methods can be used for the outer optimization: may be discrete set in sampling-based solver [26], or continuous action space over which policy is optimized [13]. However, solving the inner optimization is challenging, as it requires optimizing over high-dimensional noise space. Steering Imagination via Inference-time Noise Optimization. Since the initial noise acts as control variable for the generation of WMs (Sec. 2), the goal of the inner optimization corresponds to steering the WM imagination toward high-impact outcome that remains plausible under the learned model. However, because evaluating each noise requires an expensive denoising process, repeated random sampling is highly inefficient and likely to miss rare but plausible critical outcomes within limited sampling budget. Instead, we leverage the gradient of differentiable criterion function to steer generation more efficiently, optimizing the initial noise directly via gradient ascent: ϵi+1 = ϵi + η ϵi (cid:2) Ctest(oi)(cid:3) , where oi = fθ(ϵi, ohist, a), (5) where η denotes step size. Although this first-order gradient ascent requires small number of iterative updates, each involving forward and backward passes through both the WM and the criterion function, the dense gradient signal makes it substantially more efficient than zeroth-order random sampling for discovering noises that induce plausible, high-impact video generations [19, 30, 31]. Challenge: High-Dimensional Noise Space. While gradient-based optimization in (5) can steer diffusion model generation at inference time [19, 31], applying it to video world models is challenging because their noise spaces are extremely high-dimensional, e.g., 1M in driving [11] and 50K in manipulation [12]. The optimization must guide generation toward meaningful, scenedependent target events while preserving plausibility under the learned WM distribution. However, naive optimization can easily push the noise into out-of-distribution (OOD) regions of Gaussian noises, producing implausible videos  (Fig. 1)  , and the test-time criterion Ctest must be differentiable while reasoning about nuanced, task-relevant events that vary across scenes. Moreover, solving (5) is inefficient because exact gradients require backpropagating through the iterative denoising process.

sections.title: 4 STRESSDREAM: Steering High-Dimensional Video World Models
sections.content: To address the challenges of steering video world models, we propose STRESSDREAM, which defines differentiable optimization criterion by combining semantic objective using VisionLanguage Models (VLMs) with plausibility objective that prevents OOD noise: Ctest = Csem +Cpla. The semantic objective uses inference-time text specifications of high-impact outcomes to reason about whether the target events occur in the generated video (Sec. 4.1). The plausibility objective preserves the statistical properties of the standard Gaussian noise prior, thereby maintaining the plausibility of WM generations (Sec. 4.2). Finally, we approximate the noise gradient to avoid full backpropagation through the iterative denoising process, enabling efficient optimization (Sec. 4.3). 4.1 Semantic Objective: Steering towards Target Events with VLM Gradients Since video WMs operate across diverse scenes and tasks, the high-impact target events for steering may change with the policy context, requiring semantic objective that can differentiably score whether scene-dependent target events occur in generated videos. We therefore leverage the general video-understanding capability of Vision-Language Model (VLM) as the semantic objective. Given an inference-time text prompt describing the target high-impact outcome, the VLM measures how well the generated video matches the prompt. For example, in robotic manipulation, one may specify the coffee beans spill, while in driving, one may specify collision occurs. To obtain differentiable score, we use Qwen-VL [32] and prompt it to output single token, yes or no, indicating whether matches l. We then define Csem as the difference in log token probabilities: Csem(o ; l) = log pVLM(yes o, l) log pVLM(no o, l). (6) Using the VLMs single-token probabilities makes the objective differentiable, providing rich gradient signals for optimizing high-dimensional noise that generates an inference-time target event. 4.2 Plausibility Objective: Keeping High-dimensional Noise in the Typical Set Diffusion models are trained to generate samples from noise drawn from Gaussian prior. When the noise leaves the typical set [33, 34, 35], where most training-time noise samples lie, the resulting outof-distribution (OOD) noise can produce implausible videos that are unlikely under the WM distribution or have degraded visual quality. Importantly, in high dimensions, the typical set is not the same as the region of highest probability density: some noise vectors, such as the zero vector, may have high density but are extremely unlikely to be sampled from the Gaussian prior (See Appendix B.1 for more details). Since gradient-based noise optimization can perturb the noise away from this typical set [18, 19, 31, 36, 37], we employ plausibility objective that encourages the noise to remain 4 within the typical set of the Gaussian prior, with combination of functions of the noise that capture key statistics of high-dimensional Gaussian noise: Cpla(ϵ) = λ1Cnorm(ϵ) + λ2Ciso(ϵ) + λ3Cspec(ϵ). Norm Concentration. First, since the squared norm of Gaussian noise follows chi-square distribution, ϵ2 D, it concentrates sharply around the noise dimension [18]. We therefore regularize 2 χ2 deviations of the noise radius from the typical Gaussian shell: Cnorm(ϵ) := (cid:16) (cid:17)2 ϵ2 . Isotropy. While norm concentration enforces typicality at global scale, the optimized noise may still contain local correlations or structured coordinate patterns that are unlikely under an i.i.d. Gaussian prior [19]. To encourage coordinate-wise independence, we randomly permute the entries i=1, where ϵi Rk and = mk. Under the Gaussian of ϵ and partition them into subvectors {ϵi}m prior, these subvectors should behave as i.i.d. samples from (0, Ik), and therefore their empirical second moment (cid:98)Σ = 1 i=1 ϵiϵ should be close to Ik. We therefore penalize deviations from blockwise isotropy as Ciso(ϵ) := 1 (cid:98)Σ Ik2 , averaged over multiple random permutations. (cid:80)m Spectral Whiteness. Even when the noise remains typical in coordinate space, optimization can introduce frequency-domain artifacts [38]. Since Gaussian noise has flat expected power spectrum [39, 40], we encourage the noise to remain spectrally white. We aggregate into spatial-frequency bins to obtain bin-averaged powers {(cid:98)pb}B b=1. Although diffusion models are differentiable in principle, backpropagating through the full iterative denoising process (e.g., 50 steps [41]) can incur high memory cost and vanishing gradient [42, 43]. To make optimization efficient, we adopt score-distillation [43] (Appendix B.4), which approximates the gradient with respect to the noise using the gradient at the generated sample: ϵCtest(o) β oCtest(o), where = fθ(ϵ, ohist, a) = d1(cid:0) dT (ϵ, ohist, a)(cid:1), where β is scalar coefficient. This approximation only requires backpropagation through the differentiable criterion function, avoiding full backpropagation through the iterative denoising process. (7) In sum, STRESSDREAM optimizes the initial noise as in (5), using gradient that combines the gradient approximation, the semantic objective (VLM), and the plausibility objective (typical set): ϵ Ctest(o) = β Csem (o; l) + ϵ Cpla(ϵ) , (8) where the coefficients β, λ1, λ2, λ3 are tuned depending on the WM, noise dimension, and VLMs.

sections.title: 5 Case Study: Steering Dubins Car Video World Model
sections.content: We first study STRESSDREAM in controlled settings where the system dynamics and uncertainty are known. Since we know the true dynamics, this setup allows us to test whether WM steering can effectively identify high-impact futures (i.e., failures) only when they are possible under the system. Naughty 3D Dubins Car. We consider an image-based setup of discrete-time 3D Dubins car with state = [px, py, θ], continuous angular-velocity action at [1.25, 1.25] rad/s, fixed speed = 1 m/s, and timestep = 0.05 s. The naughty Dubins car introduces uncertainty by randomly flipping the sign of the control input: δt = 1 with probability = 0.2, and δt = 1 otherwise: st+1 = st + [ cos(θt), sin(θt), δtat ], δt {1, 1}. (9) Each state is rendered as 3 128 128 RGB image showing the environment and vehicle. In this setup, instead of the VLM-based Csem, we use the safety score defined as C(s) = p2 0.252, which induces circular failure set of radius 0.25 centered at the origin: := {s C(s) 0}. + p2 5 Video World Model. We train WM on an offline dataset of 4,000 random observationaction trajectories. We train safety-score predictor to regress the ground-truth score from an image, and train diffusion world model, fθ(ot+1 ϵ, ot, at), to generate one-step future observation (H = 1) conditioned on the current observation and action. The resulting noise dimension is 1024. Experiment Setup. We aim to detect action sequences that have possibility of entering the failure set using WM imaginations. We construct an evaluation dataset of 5,000 random initial state and action sequences. For each trajectory, we compute the ground-truth minimum safety score achievable under the stochastic dynamics in (9), and label it as positive if the g.t. minimum score is below zero, indicating that the trajectory can enter the failure set. We then perform open-loop world-model rollouts using only the initial image o0 and the action sequence, and classify an imagined trajectory as failure, i.e., positive, if its minimum predicted safety score along the rollout is below zero. Figure 2: Results: Naughty Dubins Car. (a): TPR and TNR for detecting possible failures in WM imaginations. (b): Overlaid nominal and steered imaginations. STRESSDREAM steers WM imaginations, detecting plausible failures that nominal imagination misses. See Appendix & Website for more results and details. STRESSDREAM steers imagination by minimizing the predicted safety-score for 10 Baselines. optimization steps. We compare against sampling without optimization: Nominal (N =1), and Best-of-N (N =10). We ablate STRESSDREAM without Cpla, and compare against classifier guidance (CG) [44, 45], which applies gradients during denoising instead of optimizing the initial noise. STRESSDREAM reliably detects failures only when they are plausible. Fig. 2 shows that STRESSDREAM accurately detects potential failures in WM imaginations, achieving high true positive rate, i.e., correctly identifying trajectories that can fail, while maintaining high true negative rate, i.e., not falsely classifying safe trajectories as failures. In contrast, STRESSDREAM without the plausibility objective in Sec. 4.2 yields low true-negative rate, indicating implausible failures are imagined without the typical set constraint. CG similarly produces many false positives by directly modifying the denoising trajectory, generating implausible imaginations. Random sampling methods (Nominal, Best-of-N) generate plausible imaginations, but are less effective at identifying rare possible failures compared to STRESSDREAM, often missing them even with multiple samples.

sections.title: 6 Experiments: Robust Policy Evaluation and Improvement
sections.content: In this section, we validate STRESSDREAM for robust policy evaluation and improvement using state-of-the-art video world models in both autonomous driving [11] and robotic manipulation [12]. Autonomous Driving. We use Vista [11] that predicts 25 future front-view camera frames at 576 1024, conditioned on future waypoints as actions, with noise dimension = 921,600. We fine-tune the model on the PhysicalAI-Autonomous-Vehicles (PAI-AV) dataset [46] and the Nexar Collision Prediction Dataset [47], which include diverse driving scenarios such as collisions. We optimize for 20 steps with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct [48] fine-tuned with Wolf [49] and X-CLIP [50]. Robotic Manipulation. We use Ctrl-World [12] trained in the DROID setup [51], which predicts 5 future frames from 3 camera views of resolution 192 320, conditioned on joint-position actions, with = 57,600. We consider six contact-rich manipulation tasks (See Fig. 7) and collect approximately 150 teleoperation trajectories per task containing both successes and failures to fine-tune the model. We optimize noise for 10 steps using Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct [52], providing the three-view videos along with prompt describing the task failure (More details in Appendix D.1). 6.1 Robust Policy Evaluation using State-of-the-Art Video World Models We first study whether STRESSDREAM enables robust policy evaluation by efficiently steering WM imaginations toward high-impact but plausible outcomes specified at inference time. As baseline, Figure 3: Nominal vs. Steered Imaginations. Top texts show the inference-time prompts describing target outcomes. STRESSDREAM steers WM imaginations toward specified high-impact outcomes that nominal generation misses. Imaginations are grounded in plausible outcomes: when target outcomes are not supported by the WM distribution, e.g., spilling sticky candies or from closed bag, STRESSDREAM does not imagine them. we compare against random generation (Best-of-N), which samples multiple imaginations without optimization with the same budget as STRESSDREAM, and selects the one with the highest score. Evaluation Setup. We generate imaginations from an initial observation o0 and action sequence a, and evaluate whether the generated video matches the inference-time text specification of the target event. For driving, we curate 100 imageactiontext pairs from the PAIAV dataset [46] across 8 safety-critical event categories (see Appendix D.3), as well as 200 imminent-collision examples [47]. Each prompt describes target event that has not yet occurred in the initial observation but occurs within the prediction horizon. For manipulation, we collect 100 failure trajectories across tasks as an evaluation dataset, and evaluate whether WM imaginations from the initial images and the action sequences can reliably identify failures (See Appendix D.2). Figure 4: Steering Driving Video World Models. STRESSDREAM steers WM imaginations toward the target events more effectively than random sampling (Best-of-N), while Cpla helps preserve video quality. Evaluation Metrics. For driving, we report target alignment and video quality using held-out evaluator, WorldModelBench [53]. Target alignment measures whether the video matches the text description of the target event, while video quality serves as proxy for plausibility. For manipulation, we label each generation as success or failure based on human judgment. (See Appendix D.4) STRESSDREAM robustly detects high-impact outcomes in imaginations. Fig. 3 compares nominal and steered generations. STRESSDREAM plausibly generates videos aligned with inference-time specifications of target safetycritical or failure events that nominal imaginations miss, enabling robust evaluation of action sequences through WM imaginations. Fig. 4 shows that, without the typical-set constraint, both target-alignment and video-quality scores decrease, indicating that the plausibility objective helps preserve plausibility. For manipulation, STRESSDREAM detects task-failure events in imagination with higher recall, whereas random generations are often overly optimistic and miss possible failures, as shown in Fig. 5. More results in Appendix E.1 & Website. Figure 5: Steering Ctrl-World: Detecting task failures in WM imaginations. Steering is grounded in plausible outcomes. We study whether steering remains grounded in plausible outcomes supported by the WMs distribution, rather than hallucinating implausible futures. To test this, we steer generations toward collision events using both the collisionfinetuned Vista model and the base Vista model, which is not trained on collision events. Fig. 6 shows that the base model cannot imagine collision outcomes even with steering, achieving lower target-alignment scores than random sampling from the collision-finetuned model. This indicates that STRESSDREAM steers only toward events supported by the WM outcome distribution, rather than synthesizing implausible events. Similarly, the right two columns of Fig. 3 show that target spilling outcomes are induced only when plausible under the WM distribution: STRESSDREAM does not induce Figure 6: Steering to collision: STRESSDREAM can induce target events only when they are plausible. 7 Figure 7: Policy Improvement via STRESSDREAM. Fine-tuning π0.5 with steered WM imaginations favors robust actions that remain successful under worst-case plausible outcomes, whereas nominal fine-tuning can propose actions where failures are plausible under the outcome distribution. Videos available at the website. spilling for the open sticky candy bag or closed coffee bag, but does so for the open coffee bag. Since sticky candies are unlikely to spill and closed bag does not expose its contents, spilling is not supported by the WM distribution in those scenes. More details and results in Appendix E.3. 6.2 Policy Improvement with Steered Video World Model Imaginations Setup. We study whether STRESSDREAM can improve robotic policy through steered WM imaginations, promoting robust actions that remain successful over the distribution of outcomes. We consider improving behavior-cloning policy, the Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model π0.5 [20], with action evaluation in WM imaginations. Specifically, we fine-tune π0.5-DROID with 40 successful demonstrations per task using weighted regression objective [13], and compare two settings: Nominal πFT 0.5 , which assigns uniform weight of 1.0 to all trajectories, and Robust πFT 0.5 , which assigns weight 1.0 to trajectories that remain successful under steering toward task failures and weight 0.1 to trajectories that fail in steered imaginations. This encourages robust actions whose plausible outcome distribution does not include failures, while downweighting risky actions that can fail. STRESSDREAM improves policies by promoting robust actions. Fig. 7 illustrates rollouts of fine-tuned π0.5 policies. Robust πFT 0.5 favors actions whose plausible outcomes do not include task failures, as identified by steering imaginations. In contrast, Nominal πFT 0.5 proposes risky actions whose plausible outcome distribution includes failures, even if they happened to succeed during data collection. Fig. 8 reports success rates over 20 rollouts per task, showing that Robust πFT 0.5 achieves higher success by proposing robust actions that avoid potential failures. Figure 8: π0.5 Rollout Results.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we propose STRESSDREAM, which steers video world model imaginations toward high-impact outcomes specified at inference-time, while remaining within the distribution of plausible outcomes. STRESSDREAM optimizes the high-dimensional initial noise of diffusion-based video world models using combination of semantic and plausibility objectives: VLM gradients steer generation toward high-impact outcomes specified by inference-time prompts, while typicalset constraints keep the high-dimensional noise typical under the Gaussian prior. With WMs for autonomous driving and robotic manipulation, we demonstrate that STRESSDREAM effectively steers imaginations while preserving plausibility, enabling robust policy evaluation by identifying actions with potential failures and improving policies by favoring robust actions that avoid such failures. Limitations. STRESSDREAM requires specifying high-impact outcomes with text descriptions, so its effectiveness depends on prompt quality and may suffer from reward hacking, where the score increases without meaningful changes in generations [31, 54], highlighting the need for generalizable and robust reward models for robotics [55, 56]. In addition, STRESSDREAM may fail to preserve plausibility or to discover relevant failures when the base WM is limited: it may steer within flawed videos produced by the WM, or fail to imagine target outcomes absent from the WMs training distribution. Thus, our notion of plausibility is limited to what the WM supports, and does not necessarily imply physical plausibility in the real world, underscoring the need for diverse robot data to train high-fidelity WMs with physically consistent predictions. Runtime also remains expensive, as current WMs take several minutes per imagination [11, 12], though this could be reduced by efficient models [57, 58, 59]. We refer readers to Appendix for more detailed discussion. 8 Acknowledgments JS would like to thank Luca Eyring for insightful discussions on noise optimization, and Lasse Peters for invaluable feedback on the writing. sections.title: A Background
sections.content: A.1 Related Works World Models for Robotics. World models have emerged as promising paradigm for robotics, enabling the prediction of future states directly from high-dimensional sensory observations conditioned on actions [60, 61, 62, 63, 26, 64, 65, 1, 66]. By generating synthetic trajectories, they have proven effective for vision-based policy learning [67, 68, 69, 70, 71, 72, 17, 13], planning in latent space [26, 73], and policy evaluation [27, 12, 15]. Advances in video generation models [41], which produce realistic and temporally consistent predictions, have further increased the expressiveness of world models in diverse real-world scenarios, especially when fine-tuned with embodiment data for action conditioning [11, 74, 12]. World models typically operate under partial observability, which naturally gives rise to uncertainty and multimodality in future predictions [75, 76, 10], particularly in settings such as contact-rich manipulation [12] or autonomous driving [11, 77, 78, 74]. In particular, diffusionand flow-matching-based [4, 5, 6] approaches have shown strong performance in modeling high-dimensional, complex environments due to their ability to capture rich, multimodal distributions over future observations [7, 8, 9, 10]. Despite this, many existing methods using world models either rely on nominal rollouts without reasoning over distribution over plausible futures [8, 12, 79] or adopt deterministic world models that cannot represent such diversity [26, 66], thereby failing to explicitly account for risks arising from uncertain futures. Inference-time Steering of Generative Models. Inference-time steering of diffusion [4, 25] or flow-matching [6, 80] model aims to guide pretrained generative model toward samples that maximize an inference-time reward [19, 81, 82, 83]. While directly fine-tuning diffusion models with reward objectives can be effective for reward alignment [84, 85, 86, 87, 81, 88], it is costly, complex, tied to specific reward [19, 83], and may shift the model away from the original data distribution [42]. In contrast, inference-time steering keeps the pretrained model fixed and instead modifies the sampling procedure [89, 90, 19, 82, 91, 92], for example through classifier guidance [25] or particle-based sampling methods such as Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) [93, 94, 95, 83]. One particularly relevant direction is noise optimization, which optimizes the noise sampled at the denoising process [96, 97, 30, 31, 98, 19]. Prior work has shown that this can improve image quality [97, 99, 42], mimic the effects of diffusion guidance [43], generate rare [18, 36, 100] or diverse concepts [101], and adapt diffusion policies in robotic control [102]. However, central challenge, especially when the noise space becomes high-dimensional, is out-of-distribution noise: samples may achieve high reward while no longer remaining in the support of the pretrained distribution once the noise deviates from the prior [18, 103, 19, 31, 104, 42, 37]. Moreover, these methods often require backpropagating reward gradients through the denoising process; in practice, many therefore rely on step-distilled models to make backpropagation tractable [105, 31, 42, 106], while recent works propose multistep score distillation to approximate reward gradients to the initial noise more efficiently [43]. Our approach adopts initial noise optimization to steer action-conditioned video world models [11, 12] with practical solution for high-dimensional noise optimization. A.2 Diffusion Models Recent generative models are often based on diffusion or flow-matching formulations, which generate samples from noise by simulating learned differential equation. This section expands on the diffusion-model formulation in Sec. 3, which provides the foundation for recent video world models capable of generating high-quality videos. We omit conditioning on (ohist, a) throughout for brevity. Forward Probability Path. Score-based diffusion models [4, 5, 24] and flow-matching models [107, 6] define generative models fθ whose sampling process can be described by stochastic or ordinary differential equation (SDE or ODE). The denoising network gτ θ , which parameterizes these dynamics, is trained to recover the reverse generative process, typically by predicting the score of the perturbed data distribution, the injected noise, or the conditional vector field. 19 Starting from clean data = pdata(o) and isotropic Gaussian noise ϵ (0, I), the forward process, indexed by diffusion time τ {0, 1, . . . , }, defines perturbed sample as xτ = ατ + στ ϵ. (10) For variance-preserving parameterizations such as DDPM, the boundary conditions satisfy α0 = 1, σ0 = 0, and αT 0, σT 1. More generally, we only require that the terminal state be noisedominated. Representative examples include the variance-preserving schedule of DDPM [4], where (ατ )2 + (στ )2 = 1, and the variance-exploding schedule of EDM [5], where ατ 1 and στ increases with diffusion time. Training Objective. θ is trained to recover the injected Gaussian noise from perturbed future observation, conditioned on the observation history and action sequence. With the noise-prediction parameterization, The denoiser gτ L(θ) = Expdata, ϵN (0,I), τ (cid:104) w(τ ) (cid:13) (cid:13) ϵ gτ θ (cid:0)ατ + στ ϵ(cid:1)(cid:13) 2 (cid:13) (cid:105) , (11) where w(τ ) is time-dependent weighting [5]. The clean-sample, score, and velocity parameterizations are linearly equivalent [5, 25]; we take gτ θ as noise predictor, and the analysis transfers to the other parameterizations. Generation via Deterministic ODE Updates. At inference time, generation starts from an initial Gaussian noise sample xT = ϵ (0, I) and iteratively maps it to clean sample by following the reverse probability-flow ODE. The deterministic update used in (3) can be viewed as first-order Euler step of this ODE, with DDIM [24] and EDM [5] as representative instances. Given the current noisy sample xτ , the predicted clean sample is obtained by inverting the forward path (10): ˆx0τ = xτ στ gτ θ (xτ ) ατ . (12) The sample is then advanced to the next, less-noisy time τ 1 by reapplying the forward path to ˆx0τ , while using the predicted noise as the noise direction: xτ 1 = ατ 1 ˆx0τ + στ 1 gτ θ (xτ ) . (13) Iterating this deterministic update from to 0 defines the generative model sampling fθ. Substituting (12) into (13) recovers the denoising update in (3). Probability-Flow ODE and Initial-Noise Determinism. In the continuous-time limit, the deterministic updates in (13) correspond to an Euler discretization of the probability-flow ODE associated with the forward path in (10) [25, 5]. This ODE transports samples from the Gaussian prior (0, I) to the data distribution pdata along deterministic trajectories. Therefore, the generated sample can be written as ϵ (cid:55) fθ(ϵ) = x0, where fθ denotes the deterministic sampler induced by the trained denoiser. This makes the generated sample deterministic and differentiable function of the initial noise, justifying the use of ϵ as control variable and allowing gradients of downstream criteria to be backpropagated through the sampler. A.3 Action-conditioned Video World Models Video world models for robotics have shown promise for policy evaluation and improvement by simulating hard-to-model systems directly from high-dimensional sensor observations [3]. In robotic manipulation, for example, world model can anticipate how objects may spill, slip, or topple during physical interaction from camera observations [71, 79, 108]. In autonomous driving, it can simulate future scene evolution in complex environments, capturing agent interactions and safety-critical situations directly in image space [11, 74]. These capabilities can support the evaluation and improvement of visuomotor control policies in simulation, reducing reliance on costly, time-consuming, and potentially unsafe real-world trials [3, 23]. Stable Video Diffusion. Recent video world models often build on video generation models [1, 2], which have demonstrated strong ability to synthesize realistic and temporally coherent videos. 20 Rather than learning from scratch, many recent approaches initialize from large-scale video diffusion models and then fine-tune them on domain-specific datasets such as driving or robotic manipulation. widely used backbone is Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) [41], latent diffusion model for imageto-video generation. SVD uses an encoderdecoder architecture to map RGB frames into learned latent space, = E(o) R4H/8W/8, where and denote the image height and width. The diffusion model is then trained to denoise latent video representations, following an objective of the form in (11), and the generated latents are decoded back into RGB video frames using the decoder. Action Conditioning. For robotic applications, video world models are conditioned on actions to enable controllable future generation [11, 12]. These actions may correspond to future egovehicle controls or trajectories in autonomous driving [11, 74], or to robot action sequences such as end-effector poses, joint-position commands, or low-level control commands in robotic manipulation [12, 22]. In the notation of (11), the denoiser is conditioned on histories and actions, (cid:0)xτ , ohist, a(cid:1) , so that the generated future is constrained to be consistent with both the observed gτ θ history and the specified action sequence. While earlier world models often learned latent dynamics from task-specific observationaction trajectories [67, 69, 27], recent diffusion-based video world models built on SVD typically start from pretrained video diffusion model and post-train it with action-conditioned data. When trained with conditional dropout, these models can also use classifier-free guidance at sampling time to trade off visual realism and action controllability [109]. History Conditioning. To generate coherent future frames that remain consistent with the conditioning observations, recent video world models incorporate observation history in several ways. Some methods inject conditioning frames by replacing the corresponding noisy latent frames with clean observed latents during sampling [11]; others use progressive or autoregressive sampling with per-frame noise schedules to encourage causal structure across time [8, 74]; and some provide sparse history frames or memory tokens as additional network inputs through attention-based conditioning [12]. These mechanisms help preserve temporal continuity, keep generated futures consistent with the observed history, supporting longer-horizon, autoregressive video generation by conditioning future predictions on previous observations or generated frames. Application: Policy Evaluation & Improvement. Action-conditioned video world models can support robot learning without repeated real-world rollouts, enabling scalable data generation, planning, policy evaluation, and policy improvement. Because current state-of-the-art video generation models are often slow and memory-intensive, they are typically used offline rather than for real-time planning. Prior work has primarily leveraged action-conditioned video prediction in two ways: (i) using imagined rollouts to evaluate or optimize policies without repeated hardware trials [15, 12, 110, 21, 23], and (ii) generating synthetic trajectories for data augmentation or policy fine-tuning [12, 111, 17, 13, 23]. In particular, recent works report that video world models can serve as simulators for closed-loop evaluation of visuomotor policies, with predicted performance correlating with real-world success rates [12]. Similarly, policies can be improved using video world models, either through reinforcement learning inside imagined rollouts [17] or by searching for successful synthetic trajectories that are then used to fine-tune the policy [13]. While our method can be applied to these broader settings, our experiments focus specifically on the value of steering video world models for robust policy evaluation and improvement. Rather than evaluating complete robotic policy in closed loop, we evaluate candidate trajectories that could be produced by policy and assess their potential failure risks. Similarly, rather than improving policies through closed-loop exploration inside the world model, we use the steered world model to identify failure-prone expert trajectories and reweight them during policy fine-tuning. A.4 Inference-time Steering of Generative Models Given generative model fθ that maps an initial noise sample ϵ (0, I) to generated sample = fθ(ϵ), inference-time optimization aims to find generations that maximize user-specified criterion C, while preserving the plausibility of the generated samples with regularizer Reg(x) 21 that penalizes generations that deviate from the original model distribution. This can be written as = argmax C(x) α Reg(x). (14) natural way to realize this objective is to adapt the generative model itself so that it samples from reward-tilted distribution, θ (x) fθ(x) exp , (15) (cid:19) (cid:18) C(x) α which upweights high-criterion samples while remaining close to the original model distribution. However, directly fine-tuning the generator is often impractical for inference-time control: the criterion may change across tasks or users, and fine-tuning requires significant computation for each new criterion. Moreover, preserving closeness to the original distribution can require density estimates that are intractable or prohibitively expensive for high-dimensional generative models [85, 112]. As result, model adaptation can drift away from the original data distribution and produce implausible generations [42, 83]. Initial Noise Optimization. Initial noise optimization instead keeps the generative model fixed and optimizes only the input noise according to an inference-time criterion evaluated on the generated sample: ϵ = argmax ϵ C(fθ(ϵ)) . (16) This formulation allows arbitrary criteria to be specified at inference time without modifying the model parameters. If the optimized noise remains plausible under the original Gaussian prior, the resulting generation is still induced by the pretrained model distribution, so additional regularization on the generated sample itself is not required. When is differentiable, the optimization can be solved by backpropagating through the diffusion sampler [96, 30, 31]. For non-differentiable criteria, prior work estimates gradients of the criterion [19], amortizes the optimization with hypernetwork [42], or formulates steering as reinforcement learning problem [99, 102]. Since backpropagating through many iterative denoising steps is computationally expensive, several methods further distill diffusion models into one-step generators to make optimization more tractable [31, 105, 42]. A.5 Typical Set of High-Dimensional Gaussian Noise The typical set describes where samples from distribution are expected to lie with high probability. It is therefore notion of probability mass, rather than simply probability density [33, 35]. For random vector ϵ (0, ID), let p(ϵ) denote the joint density of the full D-dimensional Gaussian prior. (18) 22 Inference-time Steering of Video World Models B.1 Regularizing Noise within the Typical Set of High-Dimensional Gaussian Distribution In this section, we elaborate on Sec. 4.2, where we impose typical-set constraints during noise optimization. In high-dimensional Gaussian distributions, regions of high probability density do not necessarily coincide with regions of high probability mass, where typical samples concentrate. For example, the zero vector has the highest density under standard Gaussian, yet it is almost never sampled in high dimensions; instead, typical samples lie on thin shell with squared norm approximately d. We refer to this high-mass region as the typical set. As illustrated in Fig. 9, the typical set is distinct from the high-density region, and sampling noise outside the typical set can lead to implausible generations. This distinction is important for diffusion models, which are trained to denoise samples initialized from typical Gaussian noise. If optimization pushes the initial noise toward atypical, low-mass regions of the prior, the denoiser may be evaluated on inputs rarely seen during training, leading to approximation errors and, consequently, implausible or OOD generations. Figure 9: Probability density vs. mass in high-dimensional Gaussian distribution. Noise sampled from the typical set leads to plausible generations, whereas noise outside the typical setdespite having high probability densityproduces implausible generations, e.g., humans becoming blurry or transforming into vehicles. common way to encourage typicality is to regularize the norm of the optimized noise, since Gaussian samples exhibit strong norm concentration in high dimensions. Several prior methods have found norm-based regularization sufficient for preserving plausibility in diffusion generation [18, 31, 19]. However, the norm captures only one of the numerous sample statistics of Gaussian sample. In practice, we find that additional typicality constraints are important for high-dimensional video world models. Thus, as described in Sec. 4.2, we regularize multiple statistics of the optimized noise, including norm concentration, isotropy, and spectral whiteness, each motivated by concentration properties of high-dimensional Gaussian samples. Figure 10: Examples of atypical Gaussian noise. Left: typical noise sampled from Gaussian prior. Others: noises perturbed to violate norm concentration, isotropy, and spectral whiteness, respectively. Fig. 10 illustrates examples of Gaussian noise visualized as 2D images, where each atypical sample violates one statistic of the Gaussian typical set. Violating norm concentration produces noise with globally biased values compared to typical Gaussian noise. Violating isotropy introduces local coordinate patterns that do not appear in typical i.i.d. noise, while violating spectral whiteness produces structured, non-white patterns in the frequency domain. Thus, large value indicates an atypical spatial frequency structure. This regularizer is complementary to isotropy: isotropy checks covariance after random coordinate grouping, whereas spectral whiteness checks whether energy is evenly distributed across spatial frequencies. B.3 Vision-Language Models as General Inference-Time Criterion Rather than learning task-specific reward function or failure classifier for narrow set of events [71, 79], we use VLM as an inference-time verifier that can flexibly specify steering targets across diverse scenes and event categories [115]. This is particularly useful for general-purpose video world models, whose possible futures may vary widely with the scene and action context. VLMs also provide semantically meaningful task-specific reward modgradient signals: els trained on limited datasets may fail to cover the large space of possible video generations, making them vulnerable to reward hacking [87, 31], where optimization increases the reward through adversarial or visually meaningless changes rather than by producing the intended event. In contrast, because VLMs are trained on large-scale vision-language data, they can provide more general semantic signal for whether the generated video depicts the specified event [116]. Figure 11: VLM score for driving scenes. The three videos are generated from the same scene but exhibit different temporal relationships to the truck in the ego lane. Both the Qwen-VL-based model and X-CLIP correctly distinguish these temporal relationships. Since our method relies on dense gradient signals from VLMs, we use open-source models rather than API-based evaluators [117], despite the strong scene-understanding capabilities of recent proprietary models. We adopt different VLMs for autonomous driving and robotic manipulation based on the semantic structure of each domain. Autonomous Driving. In autonomous driving, the target events we aim to steer often depend on temporal relationships among agents, such as pedestrian motion, vehicle movement, or changes in relative distance. We empirically find that off-the-shelf Qwen models [32] are less reliable at understanding driving scenes and their temporal structure. We therefore use the Qwen2.5-VL model fine-tuned on autonomous driving videos by Li et al. [49], which provides improved driving-scene understanding. We also use X-CLIP [50], which we find effective at capturing temporal relationships. As shown in Fig. 11, for three generated videos where the relative distance to the lead vehicle is increasing, unchanged, or decreasing, both the fine-tuned Qwen model and X-CLIP [50] assign scores that correctly distinguish these cases. For CLIP-style models, we define positive prompt l+ and negative prompt l. We embed both prompts and the generated video into the shared embedding space, and use the difference in cosine similarities as the steering objective: Csem(o) = sim(cid:0)evideo(o), etext(l+)(cid:1) sim(cid:0)evideo(o), etext(l)(cid:1) . Robotic Manipulation. In tabletop manipulation, our steering objective focuses on detecting taskfailure events from generated videos. We use Qwen3-VL [52] with text prompt that defines the failure condition, and compute the score from the token probability of the target response, similar to recent generalizable reward-modeling approaches [118]. Fig. 12 shows that the model score identifies task-relevant events at the correct moments in generated videos. We provide the five-frame videos generated by the world model as input and backpropagate dense gradient signals through the 28 Figure 12: Robotic Manipulation: VLM scores for video understanding.The plot shows the token probability of answering Yes to given prompt. The VLM takes videos from three camera views simultaneously and assigns high scores when the specified events occur, correctly detecting task-relevant moments. video. We find that using all three camera views simultaneously is essential for reliable scoring: for the same scene and text prompt, using single viewpoint leads to substantially poorer failureevent detection. We also find that CLIP-style models, including X-CLIP [50], are less effective at understanding these manipulation scenes. Efficacy of Gradient Approximation for Noise Optimization. The noise-gradient approximation enables efficient gradient-based steering without backpropagating through the iterative denoising process of large-scale video diffusion models. However, it relies on the assumption that the Jacobian of the diffusion sampler is approximately diagonal [43]. Indeed, prior work on noise optimization for text-to-image generation often relies on onestep distilled generators to make noise optimization tractable without differentiating through the full iterative sampling process. Figure 13: Average VLM scores across optimization steps in Vista. With the gradient approximation, STRESSDREAM successfully increases the criterion values, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approximation. We therefore empirically evaluate whether this approximation provides useful gradient signal in the initial-noise space for generating high-reward samples in the output space. Fig. 13 shows the average VLM score during noise optimization for steering Vista [11] generations in Sec. 6.1. The score increases throughout optimization, indicating that the approximate gradient can improve the outputspace VLM objective by updating only the initial noise. On an H100 GPU with 80GB VRAM, computing the full gradient through the denoising trajectory is impractical even with gradient checkpointing [119], whereas the proposed approximation makes the optimization feasible. 30 For Ctrl-World [12], where the full noise gradient can be computed on an H100 GPU using gradient checkpointing [119], we compare noise optimization with and without the gradient approximation. Fig. 14 shows that the approximate gradient successfully increases the VLM score, whereas optimization with the full gradient fails to improve it. We hypothesize two possible causes. First, backpropagating through 50 denoising steps may lead to inaccurate or vanishing gradients due to finite-precision computation. Second, while the VLM gradient is computed in the video space with relatively high numerical precision, the denoising process is typically run in lower precision due to memory constraints, which may further degrade gradient quality. Figure 14: Noise optimization with approximate vs. exact gradients. B.5 Implementation Details We summarize the overall procedure of STRESSDREAM in Algorithm 1. To improve optimization stability, we use both global gradient-norm clipping and per-coordinate gradient clipping. We additionally monitor the Euclidean norm of the noise and project it back to the typical shell, with norm D, whenever it deviates by more than 3.0. This threshold is conservative: the standard deviation of the Gaussian norm is approximately 0.707 in high dimensions, as shown in Corollary 2, so the interval contains more than 99.99% of typical Gaussian samples. In addition, we omit momentum in the optimizer, since accumulated updates can easily push the noise outside the typical set. Let Tdenoise denote the cost of one denoising step and the number of denoising steps used by the video world model. single deterministic generation costs Tgen = Tdenoise. Let Tverifier denote the cost of evaluating the verifier, such as VLM-based criterion, on generated video. (Sec.4.3) (2K Tdenoise + 2 Tverifier) Table 1 compares nominal generation, Best-of-N sampling, and STRESSDREAM under the same budget of sampled candidates or steering iterations. Compared to Best-ofN sampling, STRESSDREAM adds the cost of computing verifier gradient at each steering iteration. With the gradient approximation in Sec. 4.3, however, STRESSDREAM avoids backpropagating through the full denoising trajectory, so the additional cost is mainly the backward pass through the verifier, represented by the second Tverifier term. Without this approximation, differentiating through the K-step sampler adds backward pass through the denoising trajectory, substantially increasing both computation and memory. Here, is the number of denoising steps, is the number of sampled candidates or steering iterations, Tdenoise is the cost of one denoising step, and Tverifier is the cost of one verifier evaluation. In practice, using Vista [11] with = 50 denoising steps on single H100 GPU, single generation takes approximately 12 minutes, while noise optimization with = 20 iterations takes about 30 minutes. Since the dominant cost comes from video generation, the runtime of STRESSDREAM is expected to improve with faster video world models, such as shortcut [120, 70] models.

The world-model hyperparameters are summarized in Tables 2. STEERING HYPERPARAMETER Evaluation Setup. We evaluate steering on autoregressive rollouts of the video world model. Each rollout starts from the initial image of an evaluation trajectory and is conditioned on the corresponding action sequence. We assign ground-truth label to each evaluation trajectory using Monte Carlo rollouts of the true uncertain dynamics in (9). For each initial state and action sequence, we sample 10,000 ground-truth trajectories. We label the trajectory as positive if at least one sampled trajectory enters the failure set, indicating that failure is possible. We label it as negative if none of the sampled trajectories enter the failure set. This allows us to evaluate whether pessimistic steering identifies genuinely failure-prone trajectories while avoiding spurious failures on trajectories that are effectively safe under the true dynamics. We steer the generated rollout using the hyperparameters in Table 3. Since this setup is small-scale, we use the full gradient without the approximation. This allows us to directly evaluate the effect of optimizing the initial noise and the proposed regularizers without introducing additional approximation error from the gradient estimator. We also compare against classifier guidance [44, 45], which modifies the denoising process at each diffusion step using the gradient of the failure criterion. In the world-model setting, however, such guidance is difficult to apply directly. The failure predictor ℓθ is trained on clean latent states, not on noisy intermediate diffusion states. Therefore, evaluating ℓθ directly on zτ can provide unreliable gradients, while exact guidance would require backpropagating through the denoiser at every denoising step. To make classifier guidance tractable, we use one-step clean-latent approximation [45]. Given noisy latent zτ t+1, we estimate the corresponding clean latent by inverting the forward diffusion path: (cid:0)zτ t+1 στ gτ zτ θ ατ t+1, zt, at ˆz0τ t+1 = (cid:1) . The guidance gradient is then computed on this approximately clean latent: zτ t+1 ℓθ (cid:17) (cid:16) ˆz0τ t+ 1 ατ ˆz0τ t+1 (cid:16) (cid:17) , ˆz0τ t+1 ℓθ which avoids backpropagating through the denoising network, but it can introduce bias because the one-step clean prediction may be misaligned with the final generated latent after the full denoising trajectory. This issue is particularly pronounced in action-conditioned world models, where small guidance errors can push the generation toward high-failure latents that are inconsistent with the conditioning action sequence or the learned dynamics. Results: Pessimistic Steering. Fig. 15 reports the average minimum safety score over imagined trajectories for the experiments in Sec. 5. STRESSDREAM effectively steers imaginations toward lower safety scores that closely match the minimum achievable safety scores of the ground-truth system, suggesting that WM imaginations can provide meaningful signals for policy evaluation. In contrast, CG and STRESSDREAM without the plausibility objective predict implausibly low safety scores, indicating that they can over-steer generations beyond plausible outcomes. 33 Figure 15: Average minimum safety scores over imagined trajectories. C.2 Ablation: Optimistic Steering While Sec. 5 focuses on pessimistic steering to detect potential entries into the failure set, we also ablate the opposite direction by performing optimistic steering. Specifically, we change the sign of the steering objective so that optimization seeks imaginations with higher safety scores. Fig. 16 shows the resulting maximum safety score for each imagined trajectory, together with the maximum safety score achievable under the ground-truth stochastic dynamics as horizontal reference line. The results are consistent with the findings in Sec. 5: STRESSDREAM can steer the world model effectively, here toward optimistic outcomes, while keeping the generated trajectories plausibly bounded by the ground-truth unFigure 16: Ablation: Optimistic certainty. In contrast, removing the regularizers or using classifier Steering of Naughty Dubins Car. guidance can also increase the imagined safety score, but often produces overly optimistic generations that exceed what is plausible under the ground-truth system dynamics. C.3 Ablation: Impact of plausibility objective We ablate the impact of the regularizers in Sec. 4.2 on keeping generations plausible, or indistribution. Unlike video world models trained on internet-scale data, this controlled setting gives us tractable access to the training distribution, allowing us to directly inspect whether steering keeps generations consistent with the training-time distribution. We report typicality scores of the optimized initial noise, including the chi-square log-probability of the squared noise norm, the isotropy regularizer value, and the spectral-whiteness regularizer value. We also remove each regularizer individually to evaluate its contribution. Empirical Density Estimation. We additionally evaluate whether the generated samples themselves remain in-distribution by fitting flow-matching density surrogate in the latent observation space [121]. Let = Eθ(o) denote the latent representation of an observation from the training dataset. We train flow-matching vector field qψ that transports training latents to standard Gaussian noise. sections.title: D Experiment Details
sections.content: D.1 Video World Models Hyperparameters. Table 5 and Table 6 summarize the video world model and noise-optimization hyperparameters for autonomous driving and robotic manipulation, respectively. For the driving world model, Vista [11]1, single generation produces 25 future frames at 10Hz. The action input is an 8-dimensional vector representing the top-down future ego trajectory as four future waypoints. For Ctrl-World [12]2, videos are subsampled to 5Hz, and the action input consists of joint-position and gripper commands. Since each prediction generates 5 future frames, the action dimension is 40 = 8 H. To imagine longer manipulation trajectories, we use autoregressive generation, where the last generated frame is used as the initial conditioning frame for the next prediction window.
